feedback.text: I thank whoever takes the time read this feedback comment, regardless of whether or not they are a citizen of the EU. My name is Justin Travis Spears, and I live in the United States. I am not a citizen of the E.U., and I do not plan on pretending that I am a casual expert of the laws of the European Union. However, I think it is absolutely necessary for everyone associated with the Digital Fairness Act that this can be the first step in giving millions of people the proper foothold they need to retain their dignity as both consumers and people with basic human rights. The internet and the world wide web as we know it is, while it has impacted the world dramatically in only a few decades, has left law makers and governments across the world in conflict as to how it should be regulated. With how quickly the internet has become a necessity to daily life, it is easy to allow service providers the freedom to handle their online digital products as they see fit. After all, they own the servers, the data stored on them, and are responsible for the accounts made on their platforms. While several service providers have done consumer friendly practices involving their online platforms, the lack of legal regulation in the United States has allowed the publishers of online media including games, cinema and predatory subscription services to lease out their products within virtual marketplaces instead of selling them like any other product. The idea that data, while mostly intangible, can be revoked from the buyer has become common practice in North America, and will continue to do so. The failure to protect consumer rights in my homeland has resulted in grave consequences, and it has been accepted that the laws simply will not change regardless of circumstance. Lobbyists will buy their votes, bills will be tagged onto other bills, unpopular decisions will be made excluded from the hearts and minds of the people. While the point of no return has already been passed through in the new world, the old world still has a chance to learn from my government's mistakes. I implore any and all political figures involved to look over this proposal with greater passion than they are willing to give. I desperately ask that those that have already made their decision based on previous political alignment to reconsider, because this is not an act about politics or infringing on the free market. The moment that the final decisions will be made on this act will affect policy for decades to come, and if not then centuries. Future policies will use this point as a reference to decide how much agency the common citizen has over their purchased, legally owned property. At minimum, let the law clarified so that the E.U. understands exactly how it views consumer rights and protections against fraud and manipulative tactics used to attract more revenue. The people of the EU deserve to know for certain how their governments stand on these pressing issues that can no longer be delayed. With no fewer words can I emphasize how critical this one act can be at building the online marketplace of the future. I am a nobody from nowhere with nothing but words, and all I can do is ask. All I may do is request that this act be given its opportunity to make the lives of people I will never meet only noticeably better. I have no authority over the final decision of the Digital Fairness Act, nor would I ever want that kind of choice. If there is one piece of information that whoever reads this comment that is capable of moving forward with this act understands is that today, they can make that choice. Tomorrow, they might not. ~ With sincerest regards, Justin Travis Spears, a nobody from nowhere
